title: Mount ConfigMap as Volume Files
description:
Deploy a Pod that reads configuration from files mounted from a ConfigMap at `/etc/config/`. Each ConfigMap key becomes a file — this is ideal for apps that read config from disk (e.g., Java `.properties` files, INI files).

**Common Pitfalls:** The `mountPath` directory is fully managed by Kubernetes — any pre-existing files at that path are hidden. Use `subPath` if you need to mount a single key without shadowing the directory.
steps: Define a ConfigMap named 'app-config' with a key 'app.properties' containing multi-line text
Define a Pod named 'config-reader' using image busybox
Add a volume of type configMap referencing 'app-config'
Mount the volume at /etc/config in the container
Use command: ['sh','-c','cat /etc/config/app.properties && sleep 3600'] to verify the file
Apply and check logs: kubectl logs config-reader
hints: Use the | YAML block scalar to write multi-line values in data
spec.volumes defines the volume; spec.containers[].volumeMounts mounts it
The volume name in volumes and volumeMounts must match exactly
busybox's sh -c lets you run shell commands as the container entrypoint
Mount the config volume at '/etc/config' so the command can read /etc/config/app.properties

editor_config.initial_code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  app.properties: | # TODO: Add multi-line config, e.g. max.connections=100 and timeout=30s
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: config-reader
spec:
  containers:
  - name: reader
    image: busybox
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'cat /etc/config/app.properties && sleep 3600']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: config-vol
      mountPath: # TODO: use '/etc/config'
  volumes:
  - name: config-vol
    configMap:
      name: app-config

_solution.code_solution:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  app.properties: |
    max.connections=100
    timeout=30s
    log.level=info
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: config-reader
spec:
  containers:
  - name: reader
    image: busybox
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'cat /etc/config/app.properties && sleep 3600']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: config-vol
      mountPath: /etc/config
  volumes:
  - name: config-vol
    configMap:
      name: app-config
_solution.validation_script:
#!/bin/bash
set -e

echo 'Checking ConfigMap app-config exists...'
if ! kubectl get configmap app-config &>/dev/null; then
  echo '✗ ConfigMap app-config not found'
  exit 1
fi
echo '✓ ConfigMap app-config exists'

echo 'Checking app.properties key exists...'
if ! kubectl get configmap app-config -o jsonpath='{.data.app\.properties}' | grep -q '.'; then
  echo '✗ app.properties key not found in ConfigMap'
  exit 1
fi
echo '✓ app.properties key present'

echo 'Waiting for Pod config-reader to be Running...'
kubectl wait pod config-reader --for=condition=Ready --timeout=60s
echo '✓ Pod config-reader is Running'

echo 'Verifying file exists at /etc/config/app.properties in pod...'
if ! kubectl exec config-reader -- ls /etc/config/app.properties &>/dev/null; then
  echo '✗ File /etc/config/app.properties not found in pod'
  exit 1
fi
echo '✓ File /etc/config/app.properties exists in pod'

echo 'Verifying file has content...'
CONTENT=$(kubectl exec config-reader -- cat /etc/config/app.properties 2>/dev/null)
if [ -z "$CONTENT" ]; then
  echo '✗ /etc/config/app.properties is empty'
  exit 1
fi
echo '✓ File has content'

echo ''
echo 'All checks passed!'
exit 0
